(松友 美佐紀, *Matsutomo Misaki*; born 8 February 1992) is a Japanese [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") player who is a doubles specialist.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Alongside her long-time partner [Ayaka Takahashi](/wiki/Ayaka_Takahashi "Ayaka Takahashi"), she secured Japan's first-ever gold medal in badminton at the at the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_doubles "Badminton at the 2016 Summer Olympics – Women's doubles").[[3]](#cite_note-og16-3) The victory credited with effectively breaking the prolonged Chinese dominance in the women's doubles discipline.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Matsutomo and Takahashi excellence was formally recognized by the [Badminton World Federation](/wiki/Badminton_World_Federation "Badminton World Federation") when they were named the 2017 Female Player of the Year.[[5]](#cite_note-poty-5) Reaching the [World No. 1 ranking](/wiki/BWF_World_Ranking "BWF World Ranking"), Matsutomo became renowned for her technical mastery of the front court, utilizing sophisticated net play, rapid interceptions, and precise shuttle placement to dictate the tempo of international matches.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

**Misaki Matsutomo** (松友 美佐紀, *Matsutomo Misaki*; born 8 February 1992) is a Japanese [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") player who is a doubles specialist.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Alongside her long-time partner [Ayaka Takahashi](/wiki/Ayaka_Takahashi "Ayaka Takahashi"), she secured Japan's first-ever gold medal in badminton at the at the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_doubles "Badminton at the 2016 Summer Olympics – Women's doubles").[[3]](#cite_note-og16-3) The victory credited with effectively breaking the prolonged Chinese dominance in the women's doubles discipline.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Matsutomo and Takahashi excellence was formally recognized by the [Badminton World Federation](/wiki/Badminton_World_Federation "Badminton World Federation") when they were named the 2017 Female Player of the Year.[[5]](#cite_note-poty-5) Reaching the [World No. 1 ranking](/wiki/BWF_World_Ranking "BWF World Ranking"), Matsutomo became renowned for her technical mastery of the front court, utilizing sophisticated net play, rapid interceptions, and precise shuttle placement to dictate the tempo of international matches.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Matsutomo individual honors include several titles, most notably two BWF year-end finals titles in 2014 and 2018.[[7]](#cite_note-ssf14-7)[[8]](#cite_note-wtf18-8) Within the continental championships, Matsutomo is a two-time [Asian Champion](/wiki/Badminton_Asia_Championships "Badminton Asia Championships"), having secured gold medals in 2016 and 2017.[[9]](#cite_note-ac16-9)[[10]](#cite_note-ac17-10) Her competitive consistency is further evidenced by two silver medals at the [2014](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_2014_Asian_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_doubles "Badminton at the 2014 Asian Games – Women's doubles") and [2018 Asian Games](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_2018_Asian_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_doubles "Badminton at the 2018 Asian Games – Women's doubles"),[[11]](#cite_note-ag18-11) as well a bronze at the [2017 BWF World Championships](/wiki/2017_BWF_World_Championships_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_doubles "2017 BWF World Championships – Women's doubles").[[12]](#cite_note-wc17-12) At the international team level, Matsutomo has been a cornerstone of the Japanese national squad since her debut. Her contributions were instrumental during Japan's period of global supremacy, highlighted by the team's triumph at the [2018 Uber Cup](/wiki/2018_Thomas_%26_Uber_Cup "2018 Thomas & Uber Cup") and a gold medal at the [2018 Asian Games team event](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_2018_Asian_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_team "Badminton at the 2018 Asian Games – Women's team").[[13]](#cite_note-uc18-13)[[14]](#cite_note-agt18-14)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Misaki_Matsutomo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Misaki_Matsutomo_-_Ayaka_Takahashi_Indonesia_Open_2016.jpg/250px-Misaki_Matsutomo_-_Ayaka_Takahashi_Indonesia_Open_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Misaki_Matsutomo_-_Ayaka_Takahashi_Indonesia_Open_2016.jpg)

Matsutomo and Takahashi at the [2016 Indonesia Super Series Premier](/wiki/2016_Indonesia_Super_Series_Premier "2016 Indonesia Super Series Premier")

In 2016, she won the women's doubles gold medal at the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_doubles "Badminton at the 2016 Summer Olympics – Women's doubles") in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.[[3]](#cite_note-og16-3) She and her women's doubles partner [Ayaka Takahashi](/wiki/Ayaka_Takahashi "Ayaka Takahashi") were also honored with the Female Player of the Year award.[[5]](#cite_note-poty-5) They have been playing together for more than ten years, ever since they were schoolmates. Matsutomo and Takahashi became the first non-Chinese pair to win the women's Olympic doubles title since the 1996 Atlanta Games, giving Japan its second medal in the event after [Mizuki Fujii](/wiki/Mizuki_Fujii "Mizuki Fujii") and [Reika Kakiiwa](/wiki/Reika_Kakiiwa "Reika Kakiiwa") won silver at the 2012 London Olympics.

In 2017, in her fifth appearance at the [World Championships](/wiki/2017_BWF_World_Championships_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_doubles "2017 BWF World Championships – Women's doubles"), Matsutomo finally secured a medal by winning a bronze in the women's doubles with Takahashi.[[12]](#cite_note-wc17-12)[[15]](#cite_note-wc17b-15) Appearing in the deciding match at the [2017 Sudirman Cup](/wiki/2017_Sudirman_Cup "2017 Sudirman Cup"), Matsutomo and Takahashi failed to score a point for Japan, so they were eliminated in the semi-finals and settled for a bronze medal.[[16]](#cite_note-sc18-16)

In 2018, Matsutomo helps the national team to win the [Asia Team Championships](/wiki/2018_Badminton_Asia_Team_Championships "2018 Badminton Asia Team Championships") title.[[17]](#cite_note-atc18-17)

In 2025, Matsutomo returned to compete in the women's doubles stage partnering [Chiharu Shida](/wiki/Chiharu_Shida "Chiharu Shida").[[18]](#cite_note-18)
